Hi Kir,

This is just my opinion, and it is not very important.

There are a lot of things I find weird about Python's object-oriented model, and the scenario you just showed is one of them! :) Python is supposed to be a language that prides itself on being simple and succinct. Yet, Python forces us to declare the `self` parameter all the time in every method declaration, for no reason. When calling the super class, why require us to write `super(Child, self)` instead of just `super()`?

Java is an extremely verbose language, where you have to type in `System.out.println` just to print something to the console. Yet, in Java I can just call `super()`. In Java, all of a parent class's methods are automatically copied over to its children, so that I could just write `this.parentMethod()` instead of `super(Child, self).parent_method()`.

I find it weird, that's all. :)

--Jonathan

Why not use ``.format()`` here? E.g.

```python
msg = [
    '{class_name}: {error_str}'.format(
        class_name=exc_info[0].__name__,
        error_str=str(exc_info[1]),
    ),
    'namespace={namespace} key={key} requires '
    'a value parseable by {parser}'.format(
        namespace=use_namespace,
        key=key,
        parser=qualname(parser)
    )
]
```

That's much longer but it names each part of the string, which I believe is a good thing considering that ``exc_info[0].__name__`` and ``str(exc_info[1])`` are not very explicit.

Hi Rob, to be honest this is quite new for me. You'll need to elaborate a bit more on what the desired behaviour should be. For now, I have basically tried to conform to the existing (previous) implementation which was this:

```python
         elif orig_yangt in ["empty"]:
            if obj:
                return [None] if mode == "ietf" else True
            return False
```

So my reading of this is basically the following truth table:

obj | IETF-JSON | vanilla JSON
--- | --- | ---
truthy | `[None]` | `True`
falsy | `False` | `False`

So I have the following implementation:

IETF-JSON | vanilla JSON
--- | ---
`[None] if obj else False` | `bool(obj)`

_However_, there is a note later in the code where this diverges from the existing implementation....

```python
       elif map_val in ["pyangbind.lib.yangtypes.YANGBool"]:
            if original_yang_type == "empty" and mode == "ietf":
                if obj:
                    return [None]
            else:
                if obj:
                    return True
                else:
                    return False
```

For an IETF empty with `obj` as false then it would fall through this clause. In this proposed PR this _no longer happens_! I *think* this is safe, but I have no idea about the greater context. It appears to work :)

As per PEP-249 (DB API 2.0), cursor.close():
>    Close the cursor now (rather than whenever __del__ is called).
>
>    The cursor will be unusable from this point forward; an Error (or subclass) exception will be raised if any operation is attempted with the cursor.

In the current implementation, cursor.close() causes a state reset and further operations are allowed. This goes against specification.

In the Hive Thrift protocol, there is no notion of cursors, but only a session. Also, an operation can be cancelled and the session can be used again.

I think I prefer a separate cancel method. That way, at some point we can fix (or not) the behaviour of close to stick to DB-API specification.

Having said that, I am open to further discussion :)

bins_min corresponds to the binning used for computing the first slice
bins_max correspond to the binning used for computing the last slice.
The thing is that, if the binning starting at first point is used for the first and last slice, it produces a bias. Indeed, even if the point distribution is symetrical, the first slice will have more counts than the last slice. So to have perfect symetrical conditions, the first slice count must be using a binning starting at the first point found, and the last slice must be using a binning starting at the last point found.

A couple things here. First, you should never generally have to involve the parent object (ParagraphFormat in this case) when unit testing one of its children. So while ParagraphFormat is used in tests/text/test_parfmt.py for accessing the tab_stops object, it shouldn't appear for doing anything *with* a TabStops object. Likely it wouldn't appear in this file at all.

So the XML starts with the element proxied by TabStops, which in this case is w:pPr.

Related to this, you should always be able to get your unit object by calling it's constructor. Having to use ParagraphFormat.tab_stops is a code smell because it relies on the proper functioning of that method (so the test isn't isolated).

I believe how this works (when I was playing around with this I looked at the Transfer code) is that `marker` is always an opaque value that marks where in set of available data you are (by default it will be `0` if you have all the results in this response, or if this is the beginning of the set of results). It shouldn't ever be `null` but there's no harm in handling that just to be safe.

If there are more pages of results, that will be indicated by the `next_marker` property in the response, which will be set to a value that the caller should use for the next requests. `next_marker` will be `null` if there are no more results available.

So basically, you make your first call as `/task/:id/successful_transfers` and you'll get back a payload that will have a `marker` set to some value that you could send back with `?marker=<val>` if you want to re-fetch the exact same set of results again. If the task has no more results, `next_marker` will be `null`. If there are more results, `next_marker` will be an opaque value that you can fetch with `?marker=<next_marker>`.

`.nblast` is used wherever there are code cells, e.g. in https://jupyter-notebook.readthedocs.org/en/latest/examples/Notebook/Running%20Code.html

I didn't want to create a separate reST directive as container for a "code cell" (i.e. the combination of an `nbinput` directive and 0..N `nboutput` directives). Currently, the `nbsphinx` directives for input/output cells can be used on the top level, see http://nbsphinx.readthedocs.org/en/latest/rst.html#sphinx-directives-for-jupyter-notebook-cells.
Since I don't have a reST container, I also don't know how to get the parts of a "code cell" into a CSS container like the `code_cell` class used by the Notebook/`nbviewer`. If you know a (reasonably simple) way to do that, please tell me!
As a work-around, I introduced the [:no-output:](https://github.com/spatialaudio/nbsphinx/blob/2181601ed469f309d649732719adcb354f3240ad/nbsphinx.py#L74) and [:more-to-come:](https://github.com/spatialaudio/nbsphinx/blob/2181601ed469f309d649732719adcb354f3240ad/nbsphinx.py#L102) flags for the `nbinput`/`nboutput` directives, which translate to the `nblast` CSS class. This class is used in the last group of a "code cell", i.e. in an input cell if no output is available or in an output cell if it's the last of a group of outputs.

Since (currently) there is no CSS container class for a "code cell" in `nbsphinx`, I cannot use `:last-child`. I also wouldn't have to if there were, because then I could simply use the `margin` settings of the container.

But probably I can do this with some other CSS magic?

A couple mitigating points: (a) it's [well documented](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/pull/246/files#diff-a5b9ad5bed9db3883123a023e79faa93R21-25); (b) the realistic cases where `range == 0` or especially `data.size == 0` are surely very rare; and (c) the end-to-end effect is the same (i.e., if all you care about is "what does the `vz-histogram-chart` display as a result of my TensorFlow code?" then you'll see no difference).

If you're computing a histogram in the first place, I think you'll be more surprised about why your data is all zeros or absent than the edge cases of the bucketing mechanism.

Adding empty buckets is possible, though I don't like the fact that we'd have to add 14 buckets on one side and 15 buckets on the other, or enforce a parity constraint. (I considered this, and the parity constraint is why I didn't do it in the first place.)

I think that returning an empty set of buckets when there is no data is the most reasonable thing to do: in the singleton case, we at least have an anchor point for the datum (though the "unit size" parameter is arbitrary[1]), but creating, say, a bunch of unit-size buckets around, say, zero doesn't sound good. (Suppose that my histogram data, when it exists, is always a small probability, and so I naturally try to take the logs of all of my bucket boundaries to plot them…but now I have no data so my buckets are like `[-3, -2]`…)

[1]: You may note that the problem that I describe re: logarithms could also happen here, so a better transformation would be `x => {left: x / 2, right: x * 3 / 2}` with appropriate handling for negative values, but then we still don't have a zero case.)

Numpy doesn't always have a row-major layout, either.  E.g. if `a` is row-major, then `a.transpose()` will be column major.

Also the stride and the memory layout are the same concept: Row major really just means the column stride is 1 (i.e. rows are contiguous), and column major just means the row stride is 1 (i.e. columns are contiguous).  The restriction in Eigen is essentially that `Ref<Matrix>` requires an inner stride of 1 (where inner = column for row-major and = row for column-major).

(When comparing to numpy strides, numpy strides are in bytes, so multiply by the dtype size when looking at numpy array strides).

One thing that's slightly awkward is if you run Certbot with the Nginx plugin once, modify `options-ssl-nginx.conf`, and then run it again, it tells you an updated file has been saved and you should update your modified version despite them being the same base file. If users are exposed to this behavior of Certbot telling you to update your file despite them being the same, I could see them adding `--nginx-no-tls-updates` just to get Certbot to shut up.

There are are number of ways to mitigate this, but basically I think we want to save `CURRENT_SSL_OPTIONS_HASH` (or the original file) somewhere and check if it's different than our current file before creating the `.new` file with a warning. `/var/lib/letsencrypt` would be nice, but then you have the slightly awkward fact that `options-ssl-nginx.conf` is stored in one configurable directory location and it's original hash/contents are stored in another. Perhaps we could just create a new (hidden) file/folder in `/etc/letsencrypt` to contain this?

This is definitely one downside to doing things at runtime that I didn't think of. If we feel this is too hard/complex to solve correctly, we could go back to your original plan and get things in a sane state for OS packages, `certbot-auto`, `docker`, (and `pip`?), but I still think that's more work and causes Certbot to have different behavior depending on how it's installed.
